caveats: a: O*NET is occupation-level rather than NAICS-specific and does not provide wage-weighted task shares.
a: The BLS occupation chart covers the broader Personal and Household Goods Repair and Maintenance industry rather than NAICS 811411 alone.
rho: Census BTOS spans all industries and firm sizes and measures AI use in any business function, not durable labor substitution.
rho: Implementation depends on service-management software, parts catalogs, and usable historical repair data.
e: The injected firm count is only four and is an estimate derived through a size-distribution and margin bridge.
e: The NAICS definition excludes new-equipment retailers, but real-world company classification and mixed revenue models may not follow that boundary cleanly.
s5: Gallup covers employer businesses across industries and measures intentions rather than completed transactions.
s5: No industry-specific deal-flow or owner-age series was found.
q: No industry-specific evidence directly measures how AI-enabled cost savings flow into price or margin.
q: Revenue mix between flat-rate, hourly, warranty, and parts-driven work may vary materially by operator.
d5: The BLS series covers small-engine mechanics across industries, not NAICS 811411 service demand.
d5: The projection is employment rather than constant-quality service quantity.
o: The estimate applies to the current service basket and does not assume all future equipment preserves today's repairability.
o: O*NET describes worker tasks, not the fraction of customer demand that can migrate to self-service.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include households, landscapers, property managers, and other equipment users. Revenue comes from diagnostic and service charges, hourly or flat-rate labor, parts markups, seasonal tune-ups and maintenance, and sometimes pickup or delivery fees.
lens.excluded_activities: Retailers whose principal business is selling new outdoor power equipment, manufacturer- or dealer-captive service units, commercial or industrial machinery repair, internal maintenance departments, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Independent US firms repairing and maintaining home and garden equipment for external household and commercial customers, including lawnmowers, handheld power tools, edgers, snow and leaf blowers, and trimmers. The screened population is limited to recurring or repeat outsourced-service firms in the lower-middle-market EBITDA band.

a.bridge: O*NET describes the task content of outdoor power equipment and small-engine mechanics, while BLS reports the broader Personal and Household Goods Repair and Maintenance occupation mix. I map records, intake, estimating, scheduling, parts lookup, and customer communication to AI-exposed work, while treating inspection, disassembly, adjustment, repair, testing, and material handling as largely unexposed. The broader industry mix may overstate administrative exposure if 811411 shops are more technician-heavy; the core-mechanic task list may understate owners' and office staff's exposed work.
caveats: O*NET is occupation-level rather than NAICS-specific and does not provide wage-weighted task shares.
The BLS occupation chart covers the broader Personal and Household Goods Repair and Maintenance industry rather than NAICS 811411 alone.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted staffing and time-use study for NAICS 811411 separating technicians, service writers, parts staff, dispatch, bookkeeping, and management would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: The core service is hands-on mechanical diagnosis and repair. O*NET nevertheless identifies recordkeeping, customer problem intake, cost estimation, selling, information processing, and computer use, creating a bounded but minority AI opportunity. BLS also shows managers and office clerks in the broader repair industry.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment of small-engine mechanics to grow 4 percent from 2024 to 2034; that is an occupation-employment outlook, not constant-price demand for NAICS 811411's current service basket. I translate it into roughly flat to modest five-year demand, with a wider downside for replacement, electrification, seasonality, and more disposable equipment and upside for installed equipment and maintenance needs. Employment can lag demand when productivity changes, so the proxy may understate demand growth if technician productivity rises and may overstate it if labor scarcity constrains service volume.
caveats: The BLS series covers small-engine mechanics across industries, not NAICS 811411 service demand.
The projection is employment rather than constant-quality service quantity.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted five-year same-shop repair orders and revenue by equipment type, paired with installed-base, replacement, and warranty-claim data, would materially change this ratio.
d5.rationale: The occupational outlook supports neither rapid secular growth nor collapse. Installed outdoor equipment, seasonal maintenance, and repair economics support continuity, while product replacement and changing engine technology create downside.

caveats: The injected firm count is only four and is an estimate derived through a size-distribution and margin bridge.
The NAICS definition excludes new-equipment retailers, but real-world company classification and mixed revenue models may not follow that boundary cleanly.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the four dataset-indicated lower-middle-market firms, using revenue mix, customer mix, ownership, repeat-service share, and normalized EBITDA, would directly resolve eligibility.
e.rationale: Many independent firms classified in 811411 provide repeat external repair and maintenance, but some lower-middle-market firms may be dominated by equipment retail, captive dealer economics, warranty arrangements, unusually seasonal demand, or owner-dependent operations that do not fit the frozen lens cleanly.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 811411 ($267,037,783) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 811411 ($837,660,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[81]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $183,899 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 811411); class <5: 1,410 firms, avg revenue/firm $295,506 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $46,247 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 220 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,018,168 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $159,343 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 37 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,112,568 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $330,617 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 10 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,528,100 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $708,648 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 4 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,543,750 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,963,097 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 4 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,844,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $914,664 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 4

o.bridge: O*NET task content shows that current small-engine service requires inspection, physical disassembly, adjustment, parts replacement, reassembly, testing, and material handling. I map those physical accountability requirements to continued need for a repair operator in year five, while allowing some loss to self-service, replacement instead of repair, and manufacturer or retailer channels. The occupation evidence may overstate operator-required share if product self-diagnostics or modular replacement advance rapidly, and may understate it where safety and warranty requirements reinforce professional service.
caveats: The estimate applies to the current service basket and does not assume all future equipment preserves today's repairability.
O*NET describes worker tasks, not the fraction of customer demand that can migrate to self-service.
o.change_evidence: Equipment-manufacturer data on remote diagnostics, modular self-repair, warranty-channel shifts, and the share of faults resolved without a professional repair visit would materially change this value.
o.rationale: AI can assist diagnosis and administration, but the current service basket still requires possession of equipment, tools, parts, physical work, and accountability for a completed repair. Only a modest share is likely to be eliminated or fully self-served within five years.

caveats: No industry-specific evidence directly measures how AI-enabled cost savings flow into price or margin.
Revenue mix between flat-rate, hourly, warranty, and parts-driven work may vary materially by operator.
q.change_evidence: Customer-level invoices, labor standards, realized job times, warranty reimbursement schedules, pricing changes, and gross-margin cohorts before and after workflow automation would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Diagnostic charges, flat-rate jobs, parts markups, and customer convenience can let an operator retain a meaningful share of implemented gross benefit. Hourly billing, transparent local competition, warranty reimbursement, and repricing at renewal or repeat purchase can pass some efficiency to customers.

rho.bridge: Census BTOS measures recent and expected AI use across US businesses, not implementation of the exposed opportunity in 811411. I use that adoption baseline, then raise the base above current broad-business use because quoting, customer messaging, recordkeeping, parts research, and scheduling are commercially available workflows, while discounting for small-shop systems, data quality, and change capacity. This may overstate implementation where shops lack structured digital records and understate it where multi-location operators already use modern service software.
caveats: Census BTOS spans all industries and firm sizes and measures AI use in any business function, not durable labor substitution.
Implementation depends on service-management software, parts catalogs, and usable historical repair data.
rho.change_evidence: A 2026 baseline survey and five-year implementation plan from multi-location outdoor-power-equipment repair operators, including workflow-level production use and realized labor hours avoided, would materially change this value.
rho.rationale: About half of the exposed back-office and customer workflow opportunity could plausibly be operational within five years, but physical diagnosis and repair remain outside this primitive's exposed share and fragmented shops may implement slowly.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures the share of US employer-business owners who say they plan to sell or transfer ownership within five years, not completed qualifying control transfers among eligible 811411 firms. I discount the reported intention rate for failed processes, family or internal transfers that may not qualify, and owner-dependent shops that cannot transfer as operating platforms. The result may understate activity if aging repair-shop owners are older than the employer-business population, or overstate it because intentions do not ensure completed control sales.
caveats: Gallup covers employer businesses across industries and measures intentions rather than completed transactions.
No industry-specific deal-flow or owner-age series was found.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed transaction records and owner-age or succession-plan interviews covering independent lower-middle-market outdoor equipment repair firms would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: A qualifying transfer is less likely than a stated intention to sell or transfer. The very small eligible-firm pool also makes realized opportunity discrete and volatile.

principal_driver: Physical, repeat repair demand remains operator-required while a limited administrative and diagnostic-support layer is amenable to AI.
principal_weakness: Only four lower-middle-market firms are estimated, and replacement, seasonality, mixed retail models, and owner dependence can sharply narrow the actionable pool.

sources.supports: Reports that 22 percent of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years.

title: Home and Garden Equipment Repair and Maintenance
